text: Here we have a bullous lung tissue, very injured, which leaks air and you can see the rough places over the lung, which are air sacs.
Some of them are ruptured and leaking.
We put the first stapler on the lung and
shot it and you can see this tape line there which doesn't leak air and now we are trying to put the

other stapler on the lung but the lung is very airy and thick and that's why in order to put the staple we should use a clamp which will squeeze the lung and prepare a line for the second and third staple to be placed.
As you can see, it's really hard to close the staple as the lung is still thick, not thin enough to put the staple on.
So we try more than once to squeeze the lung.

in order to put the staples and you can see now we're shooting it and you can see that it's really hard to take it off as it's really still thick and keeping the lung there and we are trying to be patient and in these cases the lung are really injured and you have to be very patient
and put the staples accordingly and safely in order not to have any air leakage after the surgery.
That's why be very patient and keep an eye on the lung.
And I think it's going to be over and we will put the staple and get the injured lung out.
You can see we have some air bleeding and we cauterized there and we finished the surgery after all of these interventions.
